title: Ensure that the config file permissions are set to 644 or more restrictive

input_row.title: Ensure that the config file permissions are set to 644 or more restrictive
input_row.description: Ensure that the config file has permissions of 644 or more restrictive.
input_row.rationale: The config file controls various parameters that set the behavior of various components of the master node. You should restrict its file permissions to maintain the integrity of the file. The file should be writable by only the administrators on the system.
input_row.audit: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the master node. For example, stat -c %a /etc/kubernetes/config Verify that the permissions are 644 or more restrictive.
input_row.remediation: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the master node. For example, chmod 644 /etc/kubernetes/config Impact: None Default Value: By default, config file has permissions of 644. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kube-apiserver/   93 | P a g e
input_row.impact: None Default Value: By default, config file has permissions of 644.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Manual

**APPROACH DECISION:**
This control requires verification of the file system permissions of the Kubernetes config file (e.g., /etc/kubernetes/config) on the master node. File system permissions are not exposed via the Kubernetes API, kubectl, or any Kubernetes controller or resource. The audit instructions explicitly require running OS-level commands (e.g., stat -c %a /etc/kubernetes/config) directly on the node. Therefore, this check cannot be automated via Kubernetes-native APIs or kubectl and requires direct host access.

**MANUAL STEPS:**
1. SSH into each Kubernetes master node.
2. Locate the Kubernetes config file (commonly /etc/kubernetes/config, but may vary by installation).
3. Run the following command to check file permissions:
   ```
   stat -c %a /etc/kubernetes/config
   ```
   (Replace the path with the actual config file location if different.)
4. Verify that the output is 644 or a more restrictive value (e.g., 640, 600).
5. Confirm that only the file owner (typically root or a designated admin user) has write permissions, and that group/others do not have write access.
6. If the permissions are more permissive than 644, remediate by running:
   ```
   chmod 644 /etc/kubernetes/config
   ```
7. Document the results and confirm compliance for each master node.

**SOURCE:** CIS KUBERNETES 1.6 BENCHMARK V1.0.0 ARCHIVE
